planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in moist, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in moist but well-drained soil in full sun. This deciduous East Asian oak carries slender, sharply serrated leaves and is a mainstay of Japanese secondary woodlands. It grows quickly and colors soft yellow-brown in fall.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in moist, well-drained soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in sun to part shade. This graceful evergreen oak from China and Japan has narrow, bamboo-like leaves that emerge bronze-purple before turning glossy green. Its neat, upright form suits screens and small landscapes.

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery stock in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set nursery-grown trees with the root flare at grade in well-drained soil in full sun. This narrow, columnar hybrid of swamp white and English oak is prized for its upright form and mildew-resistant, glossy green foliage. It tolerates urban conditions and tight spaces.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring or fall in well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set trees with the root flare level in well-drained soil in full sun. This very slender, columnar oak hybrid excels as a narrow accent or screen in tight urban lots. Its disease-resistant foliage stays clean through summer and turns yellow-brown in fall.

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery trees in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set trees with the root flare at grade in well-drained soil in full sun. This upright, columnar hybrid of white and English oak offers a narrow habit and rich rusty-red fall color. Its mildew-resistant foliage makes it a reliable street and screening tree.

planting.note: Sow acorns after fall rains in fast-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in coarse, well-drained soil and avoid summer water once rooted. This dense evergreen shrub-oak of California chaparral bears small, spiny-toothed leaves. It is extremely drought-tolerant and useful for dry hedging and wildlife cover.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in lean, rocky, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in poor, gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This tough evergreen shrub-oak tolerates serpentine soils that defeat most plants, holding small, curled, leathery leaves. It is exceptionally drought-hardy and low-maintenance.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in gritty, well-drained mountain soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in coarse, well-drained soil in full sun. This low, spreading evergreen oak of high western slopes forms a knee-high mat of small, blue-green leaves. Cold-hardy and drought-tolerant, it works as a rugged groundcover shrub.

planting.note: Sow acorns in spring in deep, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in deep, well-drained soil in full sun. This fast-growing, semi-evergreen oak from Mexico and south Texas holds handsome, leathery leaves nearly year-round in mild climates. It is heat-tolerant and a popular quick shade tree.

planting.note: Sow acorns in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This striking evergreen oak from northeastern Mexico bears large, corrugated, loquat-like leaves that emerge red before turning glossy green. It grows quickly and thrives in warm, humid climates.

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery stock in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set trees with the root flare level in well-drained soil in full sun. This narrow, upright form of English oak holds its branches tightly ascending, making a bold columnar accent. It tolerates urban soils and is a classic choice for formal avenues and tight sites.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring or fall in well-drained soil with midday shelter.

directions: Set grafted trees with the root flare at grade in well-drained soil, ideally where hot afternoon sun is filtered to protect the foliage. This slow-growing English oak selection holds vivid golden-yellow leaves through spring and summer. It stays small and rounded, suiting it to garden accents.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in dry, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in dry, well-drained soil in full sun. This Iberian evergreen oak, closely allied to holm oak, produces notably sweet, edible acorns used for food and forage. It is highly drought-tolerant and thrives on hot, poor sites.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in deep, fertile soil in full sun. This vigorous oak from the Caucasus and Iran bears long, sharply toothed, chestnut-like leaves and grows into a large, broad-crowned tree. It is fast-growing and adaptable to many soils.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in dry, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in dry, well-drained soil in full sun. This small deciduous-to-semi-evergreen oak of the eastern Mediterranean is historically the source of tannin-rich galls used in dyes and inks. It is compact, drought-tolerant, and heat-loving.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in cool, well-drained mountain soil in light shade.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in cool, well-drained soil in dappled shade. This low, spreading evergreen shrub-oak of the Pacific Northwest mountains bears handsome, chestnut-like, strongly veined leaves. It suits woodland edges and cool, moist rock gardens.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall in coarse, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This dense evergreen shrub-oak of dry southwestern canyons bears small, wavy, spine-tipped, holly-like leaves. It is exceptionally drought- and heat-tolerant and forms a tough thicket.

planting.note: Sow ripe acorns in spring in gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns shallowly in coarse, well-drained soil in full sun. This small evergreen oak of dry southwestern hills bears tiny, gray-green, sometimes toothed leaves and forms a compact shrub or small tree. It withstands intense heat and drought.

planting.note: Set out balled or bare-root stock in early spring while the tree is still dormant.

directions: Plant so the root flare sits level with the surrounding grade and water deeply through the first two summers. This glossy-leaved hybrid resists the aphid honeydew that plagues other lindens, making it cleaner near patios. Fragrant pale yellow flowers open in early summer once the tree is well established.

planting.note: Transplant dormant nursery trees in early spring or during mild fall weather.

directions: Dig a wide hole, keep the graft union above soil, and firm the earth to remove air pockets. Prune any basal suckers each spring, as this hybrid is prone to sprouting from the trunk base. Sweet-scented summer bloom draws heavy bee traffic and signals the tree has settled in.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring; seed needs a long cold stratification to break dormancy.

directions: Choose a deep, moisture-retentive loam and mulch the root zone to keep it cool. This is the earliest linden to leaf out and flower, so give it a spot sheltered from late frost pockets. Fragrant cream flowers appear in early summer above the broad, softly hairy leaves.

planting.note: Set out this grafted cultivar in early spring or fall while dormant.

directions: Plant with the root flare visible and stake only if the site is windy. Prized as a street tree for its uniform oval crown and straight central leader, it needs little corrective pruning. Small fragrant flowers open in early summer and perfume the whole tree.

planting.note: Transplant the dormant grafted tree in early spring.

directions: Give this cultivar deep, fertile soil and space to develop its dense pyramidal form. It tolerates cold prairie sites well and holds a tidy shape with minimal pruning. Large heart-shaped leaves cast heavy shade, and pale summer flowers follow once the crown fills out.

planting.note: Set out the grafted tree in spring or early fall while dormant.

directions: Plant in full sun and water through establishment; once rooted it shrugs off heat and drought better than most lindens. Leaves are dark green above with silvery-white undersides that flash in the wind. Fragrant flowers open in midsummer and attract many pollinators.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in spring; seed requires extended cold stratification.

directions: Site in well-drained soil and full sun for the neatest, densest crown. The small maple-like lobed leaves emerge with a reddish tint and turn clear yellow in fall. Fragrant summer flowers appear once the compact tree matures.

planting.note: Set out dormant stock in spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Give this slow-growing linden a warm, protected location so new spring growth escapes late frost. The leaves have a striking bristle-toothed edge and open pink before turning green. Very late, richly fragrant flowers appear in late summer to early autumn.

planting.note: Transplant dormant stock in spring; where emerald ash borer is present, plan for protection.

directions: Adaptable to wet or dry ground, it establishes quickly if watered the first season. Male and female flowers form on separate trees in early spring before the leaves. Be aware emerald ash borer now threatens this species across much of its range, so monitor for canopy dieback.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist ground; seed has deep double dormancy.

directions: This bog-loving tree thrives in swamps and wet lowlands where few other shade trees survive. Keep the soil damp, as it will not tolerate drought once established. Inconspicuous spring flowers precede the foliage, and the wood is famous for splitting into basket splints.

planting.note: Set out dormant stock in spring, ideally on limestone-derived soil.

directions: Named for the blue dye once made from its inner bark, this ash favors alkaline, well-drained sites. Look for the distinctive four-angled corky ridges on young twigs. It shows somewhat greater resistance to emerald ash borer than other native ashes but is still vulnerable.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist bottomland soils typical of its Pacific range.

directions: The dominant streamside ash of the West Coast, it wants deep, seasonally wet soil and full sun. Water generously in dry summers, as it is adapted to river-flat moisture. Greenish spring flowers precede the leaves on separate male and female trees.

planting.note: Transplant dormant trees in spring; seed often needs two winters to germinate.

directions: A large, fast-growing tree, it prefers deep fertile loam and open sky above. Distinctive coal-black winter buds make it easy to identify when bare. In Europe it is under severe pressure from ash dieback fungus, so watch for wilting shoots and crown thinning.

planting.note: Set out dormant stock in spring in well-drained, sunny ground.

directions: Unlike most ashes, this species produces showy, fragrant creamy-white flower panicles in late spring. Plant it in full sun with good drainage and it tolerates poor, dry limestone soils. The sugary sap that seeps from cut bark is the traditional source of manna.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; adapts to a wide range of soils.

directions: This graceful southern European ash carries slender, finely tapered leaflets on an open crown. It withstands both wet winter ground and dry summers once its roots run deep. Grow in full sun and give it room to spread its light, airy canopy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm-climate landscapes with occasional deep watering.

directions: A tough desert-Southwest ash, it endures alkaline soil, heat, and drought when established. The leaflets and young twigs carry a soft velvety down that gives the tree its name. Provide deep but infrequent irrigation and full sun for the best form.

planting.note: Set out in spring on well-drained, preferably limestone soils.

directions: Smaller and more drought-tough than green ash, it is well suited to rocky alkaline ground. Water to establish, then let it fend largely for itself in dry climates. It rewards a sunny site with reliable red-to-purple fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet, low-lying ground where it grows naturally.

directions: This swamp ash is named for the swollen, pumpkin-like buttressed base it forms in standing water. Keep the soil wet and give it full sun for strongest growth. Its large winged seeds are among the biggest of any North American ash.

planting.note: Fresh autumn-ripened seed germinates promptly; nursery trees plant in spring or fall.

directions: Prized for its mottled, exfoliating bark in shades of gray, green, and orange, this elm sheds flakes to reveal a puzzle-like trunk. It shows strong resistance to Dutch elm disease and adapts to poor soils. Unusual among elms, it flowers in late summer and drops seed in fall.

planting.note: Fast to sprout from fresh spring seed; can become weedy in favorable sites.

directions: Extremely tough and fast-growing, it survives cold, heat, wind, and drought on poor ground where little else thrives. It is often used for quick shade and windbreaks, though brittle wood and prolific seeding make it weedy in mild climates. Tiny flowers open very early in spring before the leaves.

planting.note: Sow fresh spring seed promptly or plant dormant nursery stock.

directions: A variable European elm of hedgerows and lowlands, it suckers freely from the roots to form thickets. Give it deep soil and full sun, and remove basal shoots if a single trunk is wanted. Like other classic elms it is susceptible to Dutch elm disease.

planting.note: Sow fresh spring seed quickly; unlike many elms it does not sucker.

directions: This northern European elm makes a broad, spreading crown and tolerates cool, moist upland sites and light shade. It rarely produces root suckers, growing instead as a single stout trunk. Purplish flower clusters appear in early spring before the large rough-textured leaves.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in spring on well-drained, upland ground.

directions: Named for its exceptionally hard, heavy wood, this slow-growing elm forms a narrow, oval crown. Mature branches develop corky wings that make winter twigs easy to recognize. It favors dry limestone uplands and full sun and casts a fairly narrow shade.

planting.note: Uncommon among elms, it flowers and drops seed in fall; sow fresh.

directions: This uncommon southern elm blooms in autumn rather than spring, giving it its name. It forms a graceful, somewhat drooping crown and grows on rocky, well-drained slopes. The corky-winged twigs and September flowers set it apart from its spring-flowering cousins.

planting.note: Set out this grafted hybrid in spring or fall while dormant.

directions: Bred for the graceful vase shape of the classic American elm, this hybrid resists Dutch elm disease and elm leaf beetle. Plant in full sun and water through establishment for fast, vigorous growth. Its glossy dark leaves turn yellow in fall on an arching, umbrella-like crown.

planting.note: Plant the grafted tree in spring or early fall while dormant.

directions: A compact, upright hybrid well suited to streets and smaller yards, it resists Dutch elm disease. Its narrow crown needs little pruning to keep clearance over walks. Uncommon among elms, it colors to burgundy-red in autumn, giving reliable fall interest.

planting.note: Set out the grafted cultivar in spring or fall while dormant.

directions: Selected for a tall, arching vase form reminiscent of old American elms, this cultivar shows the mottled exfoliating bark of the species. It resists Dutch elm disease and tolerates heat and poor soil. Give it full sun and room overhead to develop its high, spreading canopy.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; seed benefits from cold stratification.

directions: Widely planted as a durable elm substitute, it resists Dutch elm disease and forms a broad vase-shaped crown. Plant in full sun with deep, moist soil and prune early to a strong single leader. Fall color ranges from yellow through orange to rusty red.

planting.note: Set out the grafted cultivar in spring or early fall while dormant.

directions: This cultivar was chosen for its especially upright, symmetrical vase form and fast growth. It makes an excellent street tree, needing only light early pruning to establish structure. Foliage turns bronzy-red to orange in autumn on the tall, arching crown.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring; slow to establish but very long-lived.

directions: This long-lived tree develops a short, massive trunk that splits into a dense broom of upright branches. It grows slowly, so choose a permanent spot with deep soil and full sun. The small toothed leaves turn orange-brown in fall on the rounded, twiggy crown.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Smaller than its Japanese cousin, this zelkova is valued for peeling gray bark that reveals orange patches. New spring leaves emerge with a pink tint before turning green. Give it full sun and well-drained soil for the cleanest bark display.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; seed germinates after cold stratification.

directions: A durable Mediterranean shade tree, it tolerates heat, drought, and urban conditions with ease. The smooth gray bark resembles a beech, and the small dark berries are relished by birds. Plant in full sun and it needs little care once its deep roots establish.

planting.note: Set out dormant stock in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: This East Asian hackberry makes a broad, rounded shade tree tolerant of pollution and poor soil. Its glossy leaves and small orange-red fruits attract birds in late summer. Plant in full sun and give it space to spread its wide, low canopy.

planting.note: Plant the grafted cultivar in spring or fall while dormant.

directions: Selected for its dense, uniform crown and thick leathery leaves, this cultivar resists the witches'-broom that mars the wild species. It is exceptionally tough in wind, drought, and cold, making it a dependable prairie shade tree. Plant in full sun and it establishes with little fuss.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring; also roots readily from hardwood cuttings.

directions: This hybrid plane is the classic urban street tree, prized for flaking camouflage bark and tolerance of pavement and pollution. Give it deep soil and full sun with plenty of overhead room for its massive crown. It resists the anthracnose that troubles native sycamores and drops round seed balls that persist through winter.

planting.note: Set out dormant trees in spring in deep, moist ground.

directions: One of the longest-lived trees of the Old World, it forms a huge spreading crown with deeply lobed leaves. Give it deep, moisture-retentive soil near water and full sun for the grandest growth. It carries its round seed heads in strings of two to several along each stalk.

planting.note: Plant in spring along watercourses or where deep moisture is available.

directions: Native to California streambanks, it develops picturesque leaning, often multiple trunks with pale mottled bark. It wants deep soil moisture and full sun and grows fast where its roots reach water. Strings of several bristly seed balls hang along each stem through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring near canyon streams or with supplemental irrigation.

directions: A riparian tree of the desert Southwest, it lines canyon streams with smooth white upper bark and huge deeply cut leaves. Provide steady deep moisture and full sun, as it depends on year-round groundwater. Its seed balls hang several to a stalk, distinguishing it from eastern sycamore.

planting.note: Set out the rooted cultivar in spring or fall while dormant.

directions: This widely planted selection was chosen for strong anthracnose resistance and fast, uniform growth. Plant in full sun with ample root room and it shrugs off compacted urban soil. The flaking cream-and-olive bark and winter seed balls give year-round interest.

planting.note: Set balled or bare-root trees as soon as the soil can be worked in spring.

directions: Plant with the root flare at grade in cool, moist, well-drained soil and keep the root zone shaded with mulch. Water deeply through dry spells, as this species resents heat and drought. Yellow-brown catkins expand in early spring before the leaves, and the chalky white bark peels in papery sheets within a few years.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall while the tree is dormant.

directions: Site in moist, well-drained soil where the graceful weeping branch tips can be appreciated. Provide even moisture to reduce stress from bronze birch borer in warm climates. Drooping yellow catkins appear in spring, and the white bark darkens and furrows near the base as the tree ages.

planting.note: Set out grafted nursery stock in spring or fall.

directions: This grafted, dome-shaped selection cascades to the ground, so give it room to spread and keep the soil cool and moist. Its ultimate height depends on how high the graft was made on the standard. Small spring catkins hang among the strongly weeping branches, and white bark shows through the fine-textured canopy.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery trees in early spring or autumn.

directions: Grown for its deeply dissected, lacy leaves and pendulous branch tips over striking white bark. Keep the root zone mulched and irrigated in summer heat to limit borer damage. Yellow catkins open in spring before the finely cut foliage fully expands.

planting.note: Sow stratified seed or plant young trees in spring.

directions: A tough pioneer tree that tolerates poor, dry, or sterile soils where other birches fail. Often grows with several leaning trunks whose grayish-white bark does not peel readily. Slender catkins appear in spring, and the triangular leaves flutter in the slightest breeze.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown trees in spring or fall.

directions: A pyramidal selection prized for chalk-white non-peeling bark and improved heat and borer tolerance. Plant in moist, well-drained soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Small catkins open in early spring, and the dark green leaves turn yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring for the best establishment.

directions: Renowned for some of the whitest bark of any birch, striking against a dark background or in winter. Grow in moist, well-drained soil with a cool, mulched root run. Long yellow catkins drape the branches in spring before the leaves emerge.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Grown chiefly for its coppery-orange to pinkish peeling bark that glows in low winter light. Give it a sheltered spot with cool, evenly moist soil and light afternoon shade in hot regions. Slender catkins open in spring as the pointed leaves unfold.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: A hardy Asian birch with creamy-pink to orange bark that peels in horizontal bands. Plant in cool, moist, well-drained soil and shelter from drying winds. Yellow-brown catkins appear in spring, and the corky, warty shoots add winter interest.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist ground.

directions: A low, spreading arctic shrub suited to cold bogs, rock gardens, and damp peaty soils. Give it full sun and never let the roots dry out. Tiny upright catkins form in spring, and the small round leaves turn warm red tones in autumn.

planting.note: Plant near streams or moist ground in spring.

directions: A western native often forming multi-stemmed clumps along streambanks and wet meadows. It thrives in moist to wet soil and tolerates seasonal flooding. The shiny, dark reddish-brown bark does not peel, and drooping catkins open in spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: The largest-leaved birch, with heart-shaped foliage and orange-gray to white peeling bark. Give it moist, well-drained soil and room for its broad crown. Long pendulous catkins appear in spring, and leaves turn clear yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant young trees in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: A majestic, long-lived shade tree with smooth gray bark and a dense, low-branching canopy. Plant in deep, well-drained soil and avoid wet or compacted sites. Inconspicuous flowers appear with the leaves in spring, followed by small triangular nuts in spiny husks; dead leaves often cling through winter on young trees.

planting.note: Plant grafted or nursery-grown trees in spring or fall.

directions: A dramatic form of European beech with deep purple to coppery foliage that holds color best in full sun. Plant in deep, well-drained soil and be patient, as growth is slow. Spring flowers are inconspicuous, and the leaves fade to russet-purple before dropping.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring or fall with ample room.

directions: A grafted specimen with long, sweeping branches that cascade to the ground and can layer where they touch. Allow generous space, as mature trees form broad, tent-like mounds. Grow in deep, well-drained soil; spring flowers are subtle and the smooth gray bark shows through the draping canopy.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; shelter from hot afternoon sun.

directions: A slow-growing beech whose purple leaves are edged and streaked with rose-pink and cream. The variegated foliage scorches in harsh sun, so give light afternoon shade in warm climates. Plant in rich, well-drained soil; spring flowers are inconspicuous against the striking leaves.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring or fall.

directions: A narrow, upright beech ideal for tight spaces, formal avenues, or vertical accent. Plant in deep, well-drained soil and it needs little pruning to keep its columnar shape. Spring flowers are inconspicuous, and green summer leaves turn golden-bronze in fall.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Combines a slim, columnar habit with rich purple foliage for a bold vertical statement. Grow in full sun to hold the darkest leaf color and in deep, well-drained soil. Its narrow form needs little pruning; spring flowers are inconspicuous.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring or fall.

directions: Prized for narrow, deeply cut, fern-like leaves that give a fine, airy texture. Plant in deep, well-drained soil in sun to light shade. Growth is slow and the rounded crown is dense; spring flowers are inconspicuous and foliage turns golden in autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in full sun.

directions: One of the darkest purple beeches, holding deep blackish-purple color well through summer. Plant in deep, well-drained soil in full sun for the richest tone. It makes a large, spreading shade tree; spring flowers are inconspicuous.

planting.note: Plant young trees in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A refined Asian beech with wavy-edged leaves and smooth pale gray bark. Plant in cool, moist, well-drained soil and protect from drought. Inconspicuous spring flowers give way to small beechnuts, and foliage turns golden-brown in fall.

planting.note: Plant young trees in spring or fall.

directions: A large shade tree similar to European beech but with slightly larger leaves and a tolerance of warmer, more humid climates. Plant in deep, well-drained soil in sun to light shade. Spring flowers are inconspicuous, and the smooth gray trunk is a year-round feature.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown trees in spring or fall.

directions: A dense, teardrop-shaped hornbeam widely used for street plantings and formal screens. It tolerates pruning and shearing well and adapts to a range of soils. Small spring catkins give way to hop-like fruit clusters, and the tree holds its neat outline with little care.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown trees in spring or fall.

directions: A narrow, columnar hornbeam that stays tighter than 'Fastigiata' as it matures, ideal for confined urban sites. It tolerates shearing, pollution, and a wide range of soils. Spring catkins are followed by drooping fruit clusters, and leaves turn yellow-orange in fall.

planting.note: Plant young trees in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: A small, refined tree with tiny, deeply veined leaves, popular for bonsai and small gardens. Plant in well-drained soil in sun to part shade. Spring catkins are followed by leafy fruit clusters, and the fine foliage turns orange to red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to wet soil.

directions: A fast-growing tree for wet sites, streambanks, and reclaimed ground, fixing nitrogen through root nodules. It tolerates flooding and poor soils where few trees thrive. Long purplish male catkins open in late winter or early spring, followed by small woody cone-like fruits that persist over winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates drier soils than most alders.

directions: A vigorous, pyramidal alder with glossy heart-shaped leaves that tolerates drier and poorer soils than other alders. It fixes nitrogen and makes a fast screen or street tree. Yellowish catkins open in late winter, followed by unusually large woody cones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground; a fast western native.

directions: A rapid-growing Pacific Northwest native that colonizes moist ground and disturbed sites, enriching soil through nitrogen fixation. It favors cool, humid climates and consistent moisture. Reddish male catkins open in late winter, and the inner bark turns rusty red when cut.

planting.note: Plant near water in spring; needs steady moisture.

directions: A western streamside tree that grows quickly along rivers and permanent water. It needs abundant moisture and does not tolerate prolonged drought. Catkins open in late winter to early spring, and small persistent cones follow.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet ground or along water.

directions: A cold-hardy, multi-stemmed shrub of bogs, swamps, and streambanks, valued for erosion control and wildlife. It thrives in saturated soil and fixes nitrogen. Reddish catkins open in early spring before the leaves, and the bark is dotted with pale, corky lenticels.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates cold and poor, wet soils.

directions: A hardy Eurasian alder for cold regions and wet, infertile soils, often used in shelterbelts and reclamation. It fixes nitrogen and spreads by suckers. Catkins open in early spring, and small woody cones persist through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring on moist slopes or streamsides.

directions: A cold-hardy mountain shrub that stabilizes slopes and colonizes avalanche tracks and streambanks. It fixes nitrogen and tolerates harsh, exposed sites. Catkins open with or just after the shiny green leaves in spring, followed by small cones.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown trees in spring in moist soil.

directions: An elegant selection of black alder with finely dissected, lacy leaves and a lighter, more graceful habit. It thrives in moist to wet soil and fixes nitrogen like the species. Catkins open in late winter, followed by small persistent cones.

planting.note: Plant near water in spring in warm, dry regions.

directions: A southwestern streamside tree that grows quickly along canyon watercourses and springs in an otherwise arid landscape. It needs reliable moisture at the roots and fixes nitrogen. Catkins open in late winter to early spring, followed by small woody cones.

planting.note: Root dormant hardwood cuttings or plant young trees in spring.

directions: A very fast, narrowly columnar tree traditionally planted in rows for quick screens and windbreaks. It roots readily from cuttings but is short-lived and prone to canker. This male clone bears reddish catkins in spring and produces no cottony seed.

planting.note: Plant young trees or root cuttings in spring; note aggressive suckering.

directions: A fast, tough tree with lobed leaves that are dark green above and bright white-woolly beneath, flashing silver in the wind. It tolerates poor, dry, and salty soils but suckers aggressively and is best kept from small gardens. Catkins open in early spring before the leaves.

planting.note: Root cuttings or plant young trees in spring.

directions: A vigorous natural hybrid of white poplar and aspen, larger and more robust than either parent. Leaves are gray-felted beneath and flutter on flattened stalks. It tolerates a wide range of soils and suckers freely; catkins open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown trees in spring for a fast vertical accent.

directions: A narrow, upright aspen popular on the northern plains for tight screens and vertical accents in cold climates. It grows quickly and tolerates wind and poor soils. Rounded leaves tremble on flattened stalks and turn yellow in fall; this male clone bears spring catkins.

planting.note: Plant young trees in spring; expect suckering colonies.

directions: A fast, hardy pioneer tree whose rounded leaves quiver on flattened stalks in the lightest breeze. It spreads by suckers to form clonal groves and tolerates poor soils. Long catkins open in early spring before the leaves, which turn clear yellow in autumn.

planting.note: Plant this narrow selection where a slim vertical accent is wanted.

directions: Site this columnar clone in well-drained soil with the root flare at grade and stake only if exposed to strong wind. Its upright habit suits small yards and tight beds while still producing fragrant white summer fringe. Give supplemental water during dry spells until it is well rooted.

planting.note: Transplant into rich woodland soil in early spring for a spring-blooming understory tree.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil at the edge of woodland or in dappled shade. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist during dry summer stretches. Dangling white bell-shaped flowers appear along the branches just as the leaves emerge in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant this southern native in moist bottomland-type soil in spring.

directions: Choose a moist, acidic, well-drained spot with morning sun and afternoon shade in hot climates. Keep young trees watered and mulched to mimic their streamside habitat. Deeply lobed white bells open in mid to late spring and give way to distinctive two-winged fruits.

planting.note: Plant this pink-flowered selection in dappled shade for spring color.

directions: Set into rich, moist, acidic soil at woodland edges and keep the roots cool with mulch. Water regularly through the first seasons until established. Soft pink bells dangle along the branches in spring, a pleasing change from the usual white form.

planting.note: Transplant this wide-flowered variety in spring into humus-rich soil.

directions: Give it moist, acidic, well-drained soil and afternoon shade in warm regions. Mulch the root zone and water steadily until the tree is fully settled. Its slightly flatter white summer flowers and vivid orange-red fall color make it a prized specimen.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its cinnamon bark can be admired up close.

directions: Set in moist, well-drained acidic soil sheltered from harsh wind and hot afternoon sun. Keep the roots mulched and evenly moist during establishment. Small white summer flowers are followed by fine fall color, and the smooth reddish-brown bark stands out in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its peeling bark can be showcased.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil with shelter from midday heat and wind. Keep roots cool and consistently moist during establishment. Fragrant white cup-shaped flowers open in summer, and the smooth, flaking bark provides striking winter color.

planting.note: Transplant this deep-pink kousa in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil with the root flare at grade and mulch to keep roots cool. Water deeply during dry spells the first few years. This selection is prized for its unusually rich fuchsia-pink bracts that hold color well into early summer heat.

planting.note: Plant this compact variegated kousa in spring in part shade in hot climates.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained soil and give afternoon shade to protect the white leaf margins from scorch. Keep evenly moist and mulched during establishment. Creamy white bracts appear in late spring over the striking green-and-white foliage.

planting.note: Transplant this heavy-blooming kousa in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained, organic-rich soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Water during summer dry periods until roots are deep and established. Known for smothering itself in creamy-white bracts in early summer, followed by raspberry-like red fruit.

planting.note: Plant this pink-bracted kousa in spring with afternoon shade in the South.

directions: Set in moist, well-drained acidic soil and shelter from hot afternoon sun to keep bract color from fading. Mulch and water steadily during establishment. Soft pink bracts cover the tree in early summer and deepen in cooler climates.

planting.note: Plant this gold-centered variegated kousa in spring in part shade.

directions: Choose moist, well-drained soil with some afternoon shade to protect the variegated foliage. Keep the root zone mulched and watered through dry weather. Each leaf carries a bright gold central blotch, and white bracts stud the branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf kousa in spring in small gardens or large containers.

directions: Set into well-drained, organic soil and mulch to keep the compact roots cool. Water during dry spells, especially in containers. This slow, dwarf form fits tight spaces yet still bears abundant white bracts in early summer.

planting.note: Transplant this vigorous, large-bracted kousa in spring.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil in full sun to light shade with the flare at grade. Mulch and water deeply until established. This selection is noted for large white bracts and a strong, upright-vase growth habit.

planting.note: Plant this variegated dogwood in spring with afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained acidic soil and provide dappled or afternoon shade to prevent leaf scorch on the gold-margined foliage. Mulch and water evenly to reduce stress. White bracts open in spring, and the variegated leaves turn red and purple in fall.

planting.note: Plant this weeping dogwood in spring as a specimen where its form shows.

directions: Choose moist, well-drained acidic soil and a sheltered spot with morning sun. Mulch the root zone and water during dry weather to keep the tree vigorous. Its cascading, arching branches carry the familiar white spring bracts for an unusual weeping silhouette.

planting.note: Transplant this double-bracted dogwood in spring in dappled light.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil with the root flare visible and mulch to keep roots cool. Water regularly during establishment and dry spells. Its extra sets of white bracts give the spring flowers a full, layered look that lasts well.

planting.note: Plant this yellow-variegated dogwood in spring with some afternoon shade.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained acidic soil and shield the foliage from harsh afternoon sun. Keep mulched and evenly watered to limit stress. White bracts open in spring above leaves boldly marked with yellow and green that redden in autumn.

planting.note: Plant this disease-resistant hybrid dogwood in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil and mulch to conserve moisture. This Rutgers hybrid resists anthracnose and borers, so keep it healthy with even watering. Soft pink bracts appear in late spring, bridging the bloom gap between florida and kousa dogwoods.

planting.note: Transplant this upright hybrid dogwood in spring for street or lawn use.

directions: Set into fertile, well-drained soil and keep mulched with even moisture while it establishes. This vigorous, disease-resistant hybrid holds an upright habit good for smaller lots. Abundant white bracts cover the branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant this rounded hybrid dogwood in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Choose moist, well-drained acidic soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Water regularly during the first seasons and in droughts. This disease-resistant Stellar hybrid forms a rounded crown packed with overlapping white bracts in late spring.

planting.note: Plant this spreading hybrid dogwood in spring where its low, wide form suits.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture around the roots. Water through dry weather until well established. This disease-resistant Stellar hybrid has a low, horizontally branching habit and blooms early among the group with white bracts.

planting.note: Transplant this large-bracted hybrid dogwood in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil with morning sun and shelter from wind. Keep mulched and watered while establishing. A cross of Pacific and flowering dogwood, it bears very large white bracts in spring and colors well in autumn.

planting.note: Plant this tiered variegated dogwood in spring as a lawn specimen.

directions: Set into fertile, moist, well-drained soil and give afternoon shade in hot regions to protect the cream-edged leaves. Mulch and water through dry weather while it establishes its distinctive horizontal tiers. Flat clusters of small white flowers appear in early summer above the layered branches.

planting.note: Plant this white-edged cornelian cherry in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil of average fertility and mulch to conserve moisture. Water during dry spells, and give light afternoon shade in hot climates to keep the variegation crisp. Small yellow flowers open on bare stems in late winter, followed by red edible fruit and white-margined leaves.

planting.note: Plant this compact redbud in spring in a small garden or mixed border.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained soil with the root flare at grade and mulch lightly. Water during establishment; once settled it tolerates some drought. This dwarf, densely branched redbud has heart-shaped leaves and rosy-purple flowers hugging the stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this gold-leaf redbud in spring with afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil and give some afternoon shade in the South to keep the golden foliage from scorching. Mulch and water through dry spells while establishing. Rosy-purple flowers line the branches in spring, giving way to bright chartreuse-gold heart-shaped leaves.

planting.note: Plant this weeping purple-leaf redbud in spring as a compact focal point.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained soil and give afternoon shade in warm regions to protect the dark leaves. Mulch and water regularly during establishment. This small weeping selection combines cascading branches, purple heart-shaped foliage, and rosy-lavender spring blooms.

planting.note: Plant this weeping redbud in spring where its twisting form is featured.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil with the root flare at grade and mulch to conserve moisture. Water during dry spells until established. This weeping green-leaf redbud has contorted, cascading branches draped with lavender-pink flowers in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this drought-tolerant purple-leaf redbud in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This selection holds its glossy wine-purple leaf color better in heat than many purple redbuds, so it tolerates more sun. Rosy-purple flowers coat the branches in early spring before the leaves expand.

planting.note: Plant this vivid-flowered redbud in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil with the flare at grade and mulch to conserve moisture. Water during establishment; it becomes drought tolerant once rooted. Despite its name the buds open an intense neon magenta-pink, one of the brightest of any redbud, in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this densely blooming shrub redbud in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained soil and mulch around the base. Water regularly while establishing this compact, multi-stemmed shrub. It is famous for smothering every branch and even the trunk in deep rosy-purple flowers in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant this Mediterranean redbud in spring in a warm, well-drained site.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a sheltered, sunny position and avoid wet feet. Seed germinates faster after scarification and a cold soak, though nursery plants are simpler. Rose-pink pea-like flowers clothe the bare branches and trunk in spring before the rounded leaves appear.

planting.note: Plant this late-blooming Little Girl magnolia in spring to dodge frost.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil and mulch to keep roots cool. Water during dry spells while establishing. As one of the Little Girl hybrids it flowers later than star and saucer types, so its reddish-purple, tulip-shaped blooms usually escape late frosts.

planting.note: Plant this compact Little Girl magnolia in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil and mulch the root zone. Keep watered until well established. This small, shrubby hybrid opens deep reddish-purple, lightly fragrant flowers late in the season and often reblooms sparsely into summer.

planting.note: Plant this yellow-flowered magnolia in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Water during establishment and dry weather. Its clear canary-yellow, tulip-shaped blooms open in mid to late spring, often as the leaves begin to emerge.

planting.note: Plant this deep-yellow magnolia in spring as an upright specimen.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil and mulch around the base. Water regularly while establishing this pyramidal small tree. Rich deep-yellow, upward-facing flowers with a touch of red at the base open in spring before the foliage fills in.

planting.note: Plant this graceful serviceberry in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. Water during establishment and dry spells. Clouds of white flowers open in early spring, followed by sweet purple berries loved by birds and a brilliant red fall display.

planting.note: Plant this upright columnar serviceberry in spring for tight spaces.

directions: Set into well-drained soil in full sun to part shade and mulch around the base. Water during the first seasons and dry spells. This narrow, upright selection fits slim spaces, offering white spring flowers, edible berries, and fiery orange-red fall color.

planting.note: Set out bare-root or containerized trees in early spring while still dormant.

directions: Plant with the graft union a couple of inches above the finished soil line and water deeply through the first two seasons. Fragrant white spring blossoms give way to large red-purple crabapples prized for jelly. Prune out crossing wood in late winter to keep an open, healthy canopy.

planting.note: Best transplanted in early spring into well-drained loam.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the rootball and firm soil gently around the roots. This low, wide-spreading species blooms in a sheet of white flowers followed by tiny red fruit that persists into winter. Little pruning is needed beyond removing dead or damaged branches after flowering.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in early spring for best establishment.

directions: Site in full sun with good air movement and plant so the root flare sits at grade. Deep pink buds open to fragrant pale pink and white flowers, followed by small yellow-red fruit relished by birds. Thin the canopy in late winter to improve airflow and reduce disease pressure.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring while the tree is still dormant.

directions: Plant in full sun to develop the deepest purple foliage color and water regularly the first year. Crimson-purple spring flowers are followed by dark red fruit against glossy maroon leaves. Prune lightly after bloom to maintain a rounded form.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Dig a wide planting hole and keep the graft union above grade. Carmine buds open to rose-pink blossoms, followed by persistent red-orange fruit and good disease resistance. Remove suckers and any crossing limbs in late winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring for strongest first-year growth.

directions: Choose a sunny, open site and water deeply until established. Pink buds open white, and the bright red fruit clings on well into winter for lasting color. Prune only to remove damaged wood, doing so soon after flowering.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring, allowing room for the cascading branches.

directions: Plant where the graceful weeping habit can spread and cascade freely. White spring flowers give way to small bright red fruit that dangles from the pendulous branches. Prune to remove any upright reverted shoots and keep the weeping form.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot with room for the rounded crown to develop. Deep purplish-red flowers cover the tree in spring against bronze new foliage, followed by small oxblood-red fruit. Thin the canopy lightly in late winter to keep it open.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun for fast, vigorous growth.

directions: Give this fast-growing selection full sun and well-drained soil. Deep pink flowers open above bronze-green foliage, and glossy dark red fruit follows and attracts birds. Prune out any crossing branches in late winter to maintain the upright, spreading crown.

planting.note: Transplant dormant stock in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun with the root flare at grade and mulch to conserve moisture. Fragrant pale pink buds open to white flowers, followed by abundant red fruit that holds through winter. This disease-resistant selection needs only light corrective pruning.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in an open sunny site and water deeply during establishment. Pink buds open to a dense cloud of white flowers, followed by small orange-red fruit. Prune in late winter to remove watersprouts and keep the rounded crown tidy.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring; often sold as a grafted standard.

directions: Plant this compact, slow-growing selection in full sun where its dense rounded head can be admired. Coral-pink buds open to double rose-pink flowers, followed by sparse reddish fruit. Little pruning is required beyond removing any dead wood.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun for the best fruit display.

directions: Site in full sun and firm the soil around the roots after planting. White spring flowers are followed by masses of bright golden-yellow crabapples that persist long after leaf-fall. Prune only to shape and to remove crowded branches in late winter.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring; extremely cold-hardy once established.

directions: Plant in full sun with room for a large rounded canopy and water through the first seasons. Fragrant white flowers cover the tree in spring, followed by small red or yellow berry-like fruit favored by wildlife. Prune in late winter to remove crossing wood and maintain structure.

planting.note: Set out dormant stock in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun and water deeply during the first two growing seasons. Deep rose-pink flowers give way to elongated red-orange fruit that ages to golden orange and persists into winter. Prune lightly after flowering to keep an even crown.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun and moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. Showy double deep-pink pompom flowers smother the upright, vase-shaped branches in spring. Prune sparingly and only just after bloom, as cherries are prone to disease through fresh cuts.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring into moist, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set out in full sun with room for a broad, spreading crown and water regularly. Faintly fragrant white to pale pink flowers open before the leaves in a spectacular spring display. Prune only to remove dead or crossing wood, doing so immediately after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant this wide, horizontally-branched cherry where its layered crown can spread. Large fragrant semi-double white flowers hang along the tiers of branches in spring. Prune only lightly and just after bloom to preserve the flat-topped form.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring into full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun where its long bloom season can be enjoyed. Semi-double pale pink flowers appear in flushes during warm spells in fall and winter, then again heavily in spring. Prune only to remove dead wood, right after the main spring flowering.

planting.note: Plant in early spring, allowing space for the cascading canopy.

directions: Give this graceful weeper full sun and room for the pendulous branches to sweep the ground. Pale pink flowers drape the cascading limbs in early spring before the leaves emerge. Remove any upright shoots from the graft to preserve the weeping habit.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun; tolerates heat better than most cherries.

directions: Site in full sun and water deeply until well rooted. Carmine-pink single flowers open very early on this heat-tolerant hybrid, and foliage colors orange-red in fall. Prune lightly just after bloom to keep the upright, oval crown shapely.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring in full sun for the deepest leaf color.

directions: Plant in full sun so the foliage holds its rich dark purple through summer. Fragrant pale pink flowers open before the leaves in early spring, sometimes followed by small purple plums. Prune after flowering to remove crossing branches and shape the rounded crown.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun for the strongest foliage color.

directions: Set out in full sun and water regularly to establish this cold-hardy shrub. Small pale pink flowers open with the reddish-purple new leaves in spring. Prune after flowering, and cut back older stems occasionally to renew the foliage display.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in a sunny, sheltered spot where the very early flowers are protected from hard frost. Intensely fragrant pink, white, or red blossoms open on bare branches in late winter. Prune right after flowering, since blooms form on the previous year's wood.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Set out in spring in full sun and firm the soil around the roots. Double rose-pink flowers wreathe the bare branches in early spring before the leaves appear. Prune immediately after flowering, removing about a third of the oldest stems to encourage vigorous new bloom wood.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring; very cold-hardy once established.

directions: Plant in full sun with room for a broad crown and water through the first seasons. Small white flowers form fragrant upright clusters in spring, but the coppery-bronze peeling bark is the year-round highlight. Prune in late winter to remove crossing wood and showcase the trunk.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun for the best foliage color.

directions: Set out in full sun and water regularly during establishment. White flower clusters appear in spring on green new growth that matures to deep reddish-purple, followed by small dark fruit. Remove suckers and prune in late winter to maintain a single-stemmed tree.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with room for a spreading, arching crown. Semi-double soft pink flowers open early in spring from deeper pink buds. Prune only to remove dead or crossing wood, and do so right after flowering to reduce disease risk.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; ideal narrow form for tight spaces.

directions: Plant this narrow, upright cherry where a slim vertical accent is wanted. Fragrant semi-double pale pink flowers cluster along the tightly ascending branches in spring. Prune minimally, just after bloom, to preserve the columnar habit.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun; note this cultivar is invasive in many regions.

directions: Plant in full sun in almost any soil, as this tough tree tolerates poor conditions. Clouds of white flowers open before the leaves in early spring, and foliage turns wine-red in fall. The tight branch angles make it prone to splitting, so thin the crown in youth to reduce storm breakage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun; a common narrow street-tree selection.

directions: Plant in full sun where a narrow, upright form suits streets and tight sites. White flowers cover the tightly pyramidal crown in early spring, with glossy foliage turning red-purple in autumn. Its stronger branch structure resists splitting better than older callery selections.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun and water deeply through the first two seasons. White spring flowers precede wavy-edged glossy leaves that color red and purple in fall. Its wider branch angles give better structural strength, but thin any crowded limbs while the tree is young.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set out in full sun in most any well-drained soil. Large clusters of white flowers open in spring on a narrow pyramidal crown, followed by crimson-purple fall color. Thin the canopy while young to build a strong framework and reduce future limb breakage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun; tolerates dry sites well.

directions: Plant in full sun where the silvery weeping canopy can be admired, and avoid soggy soil. Creamy white flowers open in spring among narrow, willow-like silver-gray leaves. Prune to lift crowded lower branches and open the interior for airflow.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun to partial shade in cool climates.

directions: Set out in full sun to partial shade in moist, acidic, well-drained soil. Rounded clusters of white flowers open in late spring, followed by bright red-orange berries and good fall color. Best suited to cool northern gardens; prune only to remove damaged wood.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun to partial shade.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil for the best flower and fruit set. Unlike most rowans, it bears simple birch-like leaves and clusters of white spring flowers followed by pink-red berries. Foliage turns orange to red in fall; prune in late winter to shape.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun; tolerates chalk and exposed sites.

directions: Plant in full sun in most soils, including chalk and exposed coastal sites. Clusters of white flowers open in late spring above leaves that are silvery-white beneath, followed by red berries. Prune in late winter to remove crossing branches and keep a balanced crown.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained soil for the best berry display. White spring flowers are followed by loose clusters of pink-flushed white berries that persist after leaf-fall. The blue-green ferny foliage turns red in autumn; prune lightly to shape.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun to light shade; good for small gardens.

directions: Plant this compact rowan in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil. White spring flowers give way to berries that ripen from red to a soft rose-pink and finally near-white. Its delicate fern-like foliage colors well in autumn; prune only to remove crossing wood.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun; tolerates a range of soils.

directions: Plant in full sun in almost any well-drained soil, wearing gloves to handle the thorny branches. Showy double scarlet-rose flowers cover the rounded crown in late spring. Prune in winter to remove crossing wood, keeping the small tree open and healthy.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun for the heaviest fruit set.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; this selection has relatively few thorns. White flowers in spring are followed by large bright red berries that hold well into winter above attractive silver-gray bark. Prune in winter to lift and open the vase-shaped crown.

planting.note: Set out balled or containerized trees once the soil has thawed and can be worked.

directions: Plant this thornless, seedless selection in a wide hole no deeper than the root ball and keep the graft union above the soil line. The bright golden spring foliage matures to green, so give it an open lawn spot where new growth catches the light. Water deeply through the first two summers and prune any crossing limbs in late winter.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring while the tree is still dormant.

directions: This vigorous thornless, nearly podless cultivar makes an upright arching canopy ideal for streets and lawns. Plant in full sun with the root flare at grade and mulch out to the drip line, keeping mulch off the trunk. The tiny greenish flowers are inconspicuous, so it is grown for its filtered shade and clean fall drop.

planting.note: Best moved in early spring before bud break.

directions: A broadly pyramidal thornless form with a strong central leader that needs little corrective pruning. Site it in deep, well-drained soil and water regularly until established, after which it tolerates heat and urban conditions well. Fine ferny leaflets turn clear yellow in autumn and shed quickly for easy cleanup.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in early spring.

directions: Grown for the ruby-bronze color of its emerging spring foliage, which fades to bronze-green by midsummer. Give it full sun to keep the leaf color strong and shelter from harsh wind, which can scorch the new growth. It stays smaller than the green cultivars, so it suits a lawn accent rather than a large shade planting.

planting.note: Move while dormant in spring for the best establishment.

directions: A compact, rounded thornless selection with dense branching that casts more shade than most honey locusts. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, staking only if the site is windy. It produces few pods, keeping cleanup light beneath the canopy.

planting.note: Sow scarified, soaked seed in spring after the last hard frost.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seed overnight before sowing to break dormancy, then plant about an inch deep in a warm bed. This robust species bears branched trunk thorns and greenish-white flower spikes followed by thick pods used in traditional soaps. Give it room and full sun, and site it away from paths where the spines could be a hazard.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seed in spring once soils warm.

directions: Soak and scarify the seed before sowing an inch deep in warm, well-drained ground. This tall Asian species carries twisted pods and branched thorns and is closely allied to the American honey locust. Grow it in full sun with steady moisture through its first summers, then it becomes quite drought tolerant.

planting.note: Sow pretreated seed in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Scarify and soak the seed, then sow about an inch deep in gritty, free-draining soil. Notable for its large branched trunk spines, this species handles heat, drought, and poor soils once established. Plant it in full sun well away from walkways because of the formidable thorns.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: This golden-leaved black locust holds bright yellow foliage all season and bears fragrant white pea flowers in early summer. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, staking young grafts against wind because the wood is brittle. Prune only in summer to reduce bleeding, and remove any green-leaved suckers rising from the rootstock.

planting.note: Set out dormant grafted stock in early spring.

directions: Prized for its deep rose-purple flower clusters that hang in fragrant chains in late spring, followed by bronzy new foliage. Plant in full sun and dry to average soil, avoiding rich moist ground that encourages weak growth. Its brittle branches benefit from a sheltered site and light structural pruning while young.

planting.note: Plant the grafted standard in spring before growth begins.

directions: A dense, rounded thornless form usually top-grafted onto a standard to make a formal lollipop crown, rarely flowering. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil and stake the stem until the head is well anchored. Trim lightly after the flush to keep the tidy globe shape.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in early spring.

directions: This contorted dwarf, sold as Twisty Baby, twists both its branches and leaflets for strong winter interest. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil, keeping it small with dormant-season pruning if desired. Occasional white flower chains appear on older wood, and rootstock suckers should be pulled promptly.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: A cold-hardy hybrid locust grown for its showy deep-pink flower clusters in late spring. Plant in full sun and dry, well-drained soil where its brittle wood is protected from strong wind. It tolerates heat and drought once rooted, making it popular for tough plains and mountain-west sites.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted suckers in spring.

directions: Also called rose acacia, this suckering shrub or small tree bears large rose-pink pea flowers on bristly red-brown stems. Plant in full sun and poor, sandy soil, where its running roots help bind slopes and banks. Cut back hard in late winter to control spread and encourage fresh flowering wood.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring after the last frost.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow about half an inch deep in gritty, fast-draining soil. This southwestern native forms a thorny large shrub or small tree hung with fragrant pink flower clusters in early summer. It is very drought and heat tolerant in full sun and suckers to form thickets on dry slopes.

planting.note: Plant in early spring, as yellowwood resents fall transplanting.

directions: Also sold as 'Rosea', this pink-flowered yellowwood drapes fragrant wisteria-like chains from a broad, smooth-barked crown in early summer. Plant in spring in deep, well-drained soil and do all pruning in summer, since cut branches bleed heavily in spring. Blooming is often heaviest in alternate years once the tree is well established.

planting.note: Sow stratified, scarified seed in spring.

directions: Scarify and cold-stratify the seed before sowing about an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This Asian yellowwood carries upright to spreading panicles of white flowers and winged pods, differing from the American species in its more erect blooms. Grow in full sun to light shade and prune only in summer to avoid heavy sap bleeding.

planting.note: Sow scarified, stratified seed in spring after frost.

directions: Nick, soak, and cold-stratify the hard seed, then sow about an inch deep in well-drained soil. In midsummer it opens broad panicles of small yellow flowers followed by papery lantern-like seed capsules that ripen pink to brown. Plant in full sun where it shrugs off heat, drought, and poor soil, and prune in winter to build a strong scaffold.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring.

directions: A narrow, columnar golden rain tree ideal for tight spaces and formal accents, topped with the same yellow summer panicles. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and stake the young leader to keep the strict upright habit. It needs almost no pruning and tolerates urban heat and drought once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a strong first-season root run.

directions: This selection flowers in late summer, weeks after the species, extending the season of golden panicles into early fall. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil and water through the first two summers to establish. It is heat and drought tolerant once rooted and needs only light structural pruning.

planting.note: Sow pretreated seed in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Scarify and stratify the seed, then sow an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. Yellow summer flower panicles give way to showy rose to salmon papery seed capsules that color the whole canopy in fall. Plant in full sun in mild climates, giving it room for a broad, flat-topped crown.

planting.note: Sow seed in spring in frost-free ground.

directions: Soak the seed before sowing an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil in a frost-free spot. This subtropical flamegold bears large yellow flower clusters in late summer followed by bright pink-red seed lanterns. Plant in full sun and note that it can self-seed freely, so remove volunteers where it might spread.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after hard frost.

directions: This golden-leaved southern catalpa unfurls large soft-yellow heart-shaped leaves and, on mature trees, white bell flowers spotted purple and yellow. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil, sheltering it from strong wind that can tatter the big leaves. It can be pollarded hard each spring for oversized foliage at the expense of flowers.

planting.note: Plant the grafted standard in spring.

directions: A dense, rounded head top-grafted onto a straight stem to form a formal living umbrella, rarely flowering. Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile soil and stake the trunk until sturdy. Shear the head in late winter to keep the tidy dome and remove any suckers below the graft.
